title: Regions of British colonies
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on the regions of British colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The founders of Virginia, having discovered in tobacco a substitute for the sugar of the West Indies and the silver of Peru, still felt the lack of native labor force with which to exploit the new crop. At first they turned to their own overpopulated country for labor, but English indentured servants . . . when their terms of servitude expired . . . struck out for themselves and joined the ranks of those demanding rather than supplying labor. But there was a way out. The Spanish and Portuguese had already demonstrated what could be done in the New World when a local labor force became inadequate: they brought in natives of Africa.

From Edmund S. Morgan, historian, *The Labor Problem at Jamestown, 1607-18*, 1971.

**According to the excerpt, what caused the founders of Virginia to transition from using white indentured servants as laborers to using enslaved Africans?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Because tobacco was inexpensive to farm, plantation owners could purchase more expensive laborers
Indentured servants only worked for a short time, while enslaved Africans were forced to work for their entire lives
Indentured servants had a religious objection to exploiting tobacco as a crop, but enslaved Africans did not
Indentured servants did not have the skills to cultivate tobacco, but enslaved Africans had dealt with similar crops before they were captured

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The founders of Virginia, having discovered in tobacco a substitute for the sugar of the West Indies and the silver of Peru, still felt the lack of native labor force with which to exploit the new crop. At first they turned to their own overpopulated country for labor, but English indentured servants . . . when their terms of servitude expired . . . struck out for themselves and joined the ranks of those demanding rather than supplying labor. But there was a way out. The Spanish and Portuguese had already demonstrated what could be done in the New World when a local labor force became inadequate: they brought in natives of Africa.

From Edmund S. Morgan, historian, *The Labor Problem at Jamestown, 1607-18*, 1971.

**Which of the following contributed to the transition Morgan describes in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bacon's Rebellion
The Stono Rebellion
The Proclamation of 1763
The Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The founders of Virginia, having discovered in tobacco a substitute for the sugar of the West Indies and the silver of Peru, still felt the lack of native labor force with which to exploit the new crop. At first they turned to their own overpopulated country for labor, but English indentured servants . . . when their terms of servitude expired . . . struck out for themselves and joined the ranks of those demanding rather than supplying labor. But there was a way out. The Spanish and Portuguese had already demonstrated what could be done in the New World when a local labor force became inadequate: they brought in natives of Africa.

From Edmund S. Morgan, historian, *The Labor Problem at Jamestown, 1607-18*, 1971.

**Morgan describes how, over time, planters came to use enslaved Africans instead of indentured servants as laborers. Why did Virginia planters originally prefer indentured servants to slaves?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Racist ideas led planters to value white workers more than black workers
The terms of indenture contracts required white servants to perform more labor than enslaved people
Indentured servants were less expensive than slaves, and planters got land for each servant they brought from England
Indentured servants practiced Christianity while enslaved Africans did not

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The founders of Virginia, having discovered in tobacco a substitute for the sugar of the West Indies and the silver of Peru, still felt the lack of native labor force with which to exploit the new crop. At first they turned to their own overpopulated country for labor, but English indentured servants . . . when their terms of servitude expired . . . struck out for themselves and joined the ranks of those demanding rather than supplying labor. But there was a way out. The Spanish and Portuguese had already demonstrated what could be done in the New World when a local labor force became inadequate: they brought in natives of Africa.

From Edmund S. Morgan, historian, *The Labor Problem at Jamestown, 1607-18*, 1971.

**In what was known as the headright system, planters received 50 acres for every servant they brought to Virginia, allowing them to amass large tracts of land. Which of the following was a consequence of the headright system?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women gained more political rights in Virginia than elsewhere in the English colonies
Former indentured servants had to move  westward, where they clashed with Native Americans over land
Enslaved Africans were forced to live west of the Appalachian mountains
Former indentured servants were forced to cultivate cotton instead of tobacco, as planters dominated the tobacco market

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Of all the American Plantations his Majesty has, none are so apt for the building of Shipping as New-England, nor none more comparably so qualified for the breeding of [sailors], not only by reason of the natural industry of that people, but principally by reason of their Cod and Mackeral Fisheries: and in my poor opinion, there is nothing more prejudicial, and in project more dangerous to any Mother-Kingdom, than the increase of Shipping in her Colonies, Plantations, or Provinces.” 

>Josiah Child, governor of the East India Company, 1668 

**The ideas about trade expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Navigation Acts
The Great Awakening
The Boston Tea Party
Plantation slavery

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Of all the American Plantations his Majesty has, none are so apt for the building of Shipping as New-England, nor none more comparably so qualified for the breeding of [sailors], not only by reason of the natural industry of that people, but principally by reason of their Cod and Mackeral Fisheries: and in my poor opinion, there is nothing more prejudicial, and in project more dangerous to any Mother-Kingdom, than the increase of Shipping in her Colonies, Plantations, or Provinces.” 

>Josiah Child, governor of the East India Company, 1668 

**A historian would most likely use this passage as evidence for which of the following claims?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: New Englanders relied on fishing and shipping more than plantation agriculture because of environmental resources
British politicians recommended against founding new colonies because they were too dangerous
The East India Company wanted to outlaw British trade with the colonies because they were too industrious
New Englanders overfished cod and mackerel because of heavy international demand

assessment_items.question_content:
>"The humble, meek, merciful, just, pious and devout souls everywhere are of one religion and when death has taken off the mask, they will know one another, though the diverse liveries they wore here make them strangers." 

>William Penn, 1693 

**The views expressed in the excerpt are best seen as evidence of which of the following in Pennsylvania society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Religious tolerance
Racial equality
Democratic government
Social stratification

assessment_items.question_content:
>"The humble, meek, merciful, just, pious and devout souls everywhere are of one religion and when death has taken off the mask, they will know one another, though the diverse liveries they wore here make them strangers." 

>William Penn, 1693 

**Which of the following most influenced the ideas expressed in the passage above?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Quakerism
Mercantilism
Catholicism
Calvinism

assessment_items.question_content:
>"The humble, meek, merciful, just, pious and devout souls everywhere are of one religion and when death has taken off the mask, they will know one another, though the diverse liveries they wore here make them strangers." 

>William Penn, 1693 

**The views expressed in the excerpt are best seen as evidence of which of the following trends in the Middle colonies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Settlers came from many different countries and practiced many different religions
Settlers shared the same religion despite coming from many different countries
Settlers rarely coexisted peacefully with emigrants from other nations
Settlers incorporated Native Americans into society through intermarriage

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Image of sugar processing in Caribbean with enslaved Africans working under overseers ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ca07b15345d274cca64c4a10922a20a.jpg)
Jean-Baptiste Du Tertre, engraving of sugar works, 1667. 

**The engraving above is most likely depicting which of the following regions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Southern colonies
Chesapeake colonies
Middle colonies
Caribbean colonies

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Image of sugar processing in Caribbean with enslaved Africans working under overseers ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ca07b15345d274cca64c4a10922a20a.jpg)
Jean-Baptiste Du Tertre, engraving of sugar works, 1667. 

**The engraving can best be used as evidence for which of the following historical trends that took place during the 1600s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growing dependence on enslaved African labor
The development of a merchant economy
The creation of contracts for enslaved Africans to ensure equal treatment
The development of a representative colonial government

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Image of sugar processing in Caribbean with enslaved Africans working under overseers ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5ca07b15345d274cca64c4a10922a20a.jpg)
Jean-Baptiste Du Tertre, engraving of sugar works, 1667. 

**The creation of sugar plantation like that shown in the engraving contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Development of a mercantilist system to export cash crops
Development of a globalist trade economy to export cash crops
Development of colonial resistance to the crown to protest the use of enslaved Africans
Development of sugar substitutes like corn syrup

assessment_items.question_content:
>"We must be knit together in this work as one man, we must entertain each other in brotherly affection, we must be willing to abridge ourselves of our superfluities for the supply of others' necessities. We must uphold a familiar commerce together in all meekness, gentleness, patience and liberality. We must delight in each other, make others' conditions our own, rejoice together, mourn together, labor, and suffer together, always having before our eyes our commission and community in the work, our community as members of the same body. “

>John Winthrop, A Model of Christian Charity, 1630

**The ideas expressed in the excerpt are most consistent with which of the following practices in New England?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Town meetings
Camp meetings
Religious tolerance
Cod fishing

assessment_items.question_content:
>"We must be knit together in this work as one man, we must entertain each other in brotherly affection, we must be willing to abridge ourselves of our superfluities for the supply of others' necessities. We must uphold a familiar commerce together in all meekness, gentleness, patience and liberality. We must delight in each other, make others' conditions our own, rejoice together, mourn together, labor, and suffer together, always having before our eyes our commission and community in the work, our community as members of the same body."

>John Winthrop, A Model of Christian Charity, 1630


**The views expressed in the excerpt are best seen as evidence of which of the following in New England society?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A tradition of democratic self-government in the American colonies
The end of strict enforcement of the Navigation Acts
The extension of partial church membership through the Halfway Covenant
The emancipation of enslaved people in New England

assessment_items.question_content:
>"We must be knit together in this work as one man, we must entertain each other in brotherly affection, we must be willing to abridge ourselves of our superfluities for the supply of others' necessities. We must uphold a familiar commerce together in all meekness, gentleness, patience and liberality. We must delight in each other, make others' conditions our own, rejoice together, mourn together, labor, and suffer together, always having before our eyes our commission and community in the work, our community as members of the same body." 

>John Winthrop, A Model of Christian Charity, 1630

**A historian would most likely use this passage as evidence for which of the following claims?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: English colonizers were motivated to create societies which secured personal freedoms
English colonies were established as Catholic societies
English colonization projects were less concerned with creating community and more concerned with subjugating religious minorities
English colonies worked collaboratively with Native Americans to create a democratic community

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The one of which councils, to be called the council of state (and whose office shall chiefly be assisting, with their care, advice, and circumspection, to the said governor) shall be chosen, nominated, placed, and displaced, from time to time, by us the said treasurer, council and company, and our successor. . .
Said counsellors and council. . . [will] bend their care and endeavours to assist the said governor in erecting of the said colony in due obedience [and] in maintaining the said people in justice and christian conversation amongst themselves, and in strength and ability to withstand their enemies. And this council, to be always, or for the most part, residing about or near the governor.

From *An Ordinance and Constitution of the Virginia Company in England July 24, 1621*

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The development of chattel slavery and race-based laws
The development of a system of virtual representation in the colonies
The development of stricter colonial control practiced by the British monarchy
The development of democratic colonial governments

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The one of which councils, to be called the council of state (and whose office shall chiefly be assisting, with their care, advice, and circumspection, to the said governor) shall be chosen, nominated, placed, and displaced, from time to time, by us the said treasurer, council and company, and our successor. . .
Said counsellors and council. . . [will] bend their care and endeavours to assist the said governor in erecting of the said colony in due obedience [and] in maintaining the said people in justice and christian conversation amongst themselves, and in strength and ability to withstand their enemies. And this council, to be always, or for the most part, residing about or near the governor.

From *An Ordinance and Constitution of the Virginia Company in England,*  1621

**The excerpt above is most likely describing which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: House of Burgesses
New England town halls
First Continental Congress
House of Representatives

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>The one of which councils, to be called the council of state (and whose office shall chiefly be assisting, with their care, advice, and circumspection, to the said governor) shall be chosen, nominated, placed, and displaced, from time to time, by us the said treasurer, council and company, and our successor. . .
Said counsellors and council. . . [will] bend their care and endeavours to assist the said governor in erecting of the said colony in due obedience [and] in maintaining the said people in justice and christian conversation amongst themselves, and in strength and ability to withstand their enemies. And this council, to be always, or for the most part, residing about or near the governor.

From *An Ordinance and Constitution of the Virginia Company in England July 24, 1621*

**Which of the following describes another way in which colonists created self-governing institutions that were unusually democratic for the era?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonists in the Southern colonies gave free Africans voting rights and representation in the House of Burgesses
Colonists in the New England colonies conducted town hall meetings which elected members to colonial legislatures
Colonists in the Chesapeake colonies elected representatives to Parliament to advocate for colonial interests
Colonists in the Middle colonies elected local governors who reported to the British Parliament
